1в†’import GameServer
     2в†’import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic
     3в†’import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation
     4в†’
     5в†’/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems
     6в†’
     7в†’These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
     8в†’They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
     9в†’-/
    10в†’
    11в†’/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics
    12в†’
    13в†’These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
    14в†’equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
    15в†’about missing introductions.
    16в†’-/
    17в†’
    18в†’/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.
    19в†’
    20в†’## Syntax
    21в†’```
    22в†’exact expression
    23в†’```
    24в†’
    25в†’## When to use it
    26в†’When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
    27в†’whose type matches the goal exactly.
    28в†’-/
    29в†’TacticDoc exact
    30в†’
    31в†’/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.
    32в†’
    33в†’## Syntax
    34в†’```
    35в†’rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
    36в†’rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
    37в†’rw [в†ђ h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
    38в†’rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
    39в†’```
    40в†’
    41в†’## When to use it
    42в†’When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P в†” Q` and want
    43в†’to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
    44в†’-/
    45в†’TacticDoc rw
    46в†’
    47в†’/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
    48в†’antecedent of an implication into the context.
    49в†’
    50в†’## Syntax
    51в†’```
    52в†’intro x       -- introduce one variable
    53в†’intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
    54в†’intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
    55в†’```
    56в†’
    57в†’## When to use it
    58в†’When the goal is `в€Ђ x, P x` or `P в†’ Q`.
    59в†’-/
    60в†’TacticDoc intro
    61в†’
    62в†’/-- `cases x with | constructorв‚Ѓ => ... | constructorв‚‚ => ...`
    63в†’performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.
    64в†’
    65в†’## Syntax
    66в†’```
    67в†’cases x with
    68в†’| constructorв‚Ѓ args => tacticв‚Ѓ
    69в†’| constructorв‚‚ args => tacticв‚‚
    70в†’```
    71в†’
    72в†’## When to use it
    73в†’When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
    74в†’numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `в€Ё` have `inl` and `inr`).
    75в†’-/
    76в†’TacticDoc cases
    77в†’
    78в†’/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.
    79в†’
    80в†’## Syntax
    81в†’```
    82в†’have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
    83в†’have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
    84в†’have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
    85в†’```
    86в†’
    87в†’## When to use it
    88в†’When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
    89в†’-/
    90в†’TacticDoc В«haveВ»
    91в†’
    92в†’/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `в€ѓ x, P x`.
    93в†’
    94в†’## Syntax
    95в†’```
    96в†’use expr
    97в†’```
    98в†’
    99в†’## When to use it
   100в†’When the goal is `в€ѓ x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
   101в†’After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
   102в†’-/
   103в†’TacticDoc use
   104в†’
   105в†’/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P в€Ё Q`.
   106в†’
   107в†’## Syntax
   108в†’```
   109в†’left
   110в†’```
   111в†’
   112в†’## When to use it
   113в†’When the goal is `P в€Ё Q` and you want to prove `P`.
   114в†’-/
   115в†’TacticDoc left
   116в†’
   117в†’/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P в€Ё Q`.
   118в†’
   119в†’## Syntax
   120в†’```
   121в†’right
   122в†’```
   123в†’
   124в†’## When to use it
   125в†’When the goal is `P в€Ё Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
   126в†’-/
   127в†’TacticDoc right
   128в†’
   129в†’/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).
   130в†’
   131в†’## Syntax
   132в†’```
   133в†’rfl
   134в†’```
   135в†’
   136в†’## When to use it
   137в†’When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
   138в†’-/
   139в†’TacticDoc rfl
   140в†’
   141в†’/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `в€§` or `в†”`)
   142в†’into its components.
   143в†’
   144в†’## Syntax
   145в†’```
   146в†’constructor
   147в†’```
   148в†’
   149в†’## When to use it
   150в†’When the goal is `P в€§ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P в†” Q`
   151в†’(splits into `P в†’ Q` and `Q в†’ P`).
   152в†’-/
   153в†’TacticDoc constructor
   154в†’
   155в†’/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
   156в†’goals that can be decided by computation.
   157в†’
   158в†’## Syntax
   159в†’```
   160в†’norm_num
   161в†’```
   162в†’
   163в†’## When to use it
   164в†’When the goal is a concrete numerical fact like `2 + 3 = 5` or `7 < 10`.
   165в†’
   166в†’Disabled in levels where manual arithmetic reasoning is the lesson.
   167в†’-/
   168в†’TacticDoc norm_num
   169в†’
   170в†’/-! ### Baseline disabled tactics
   171в†’
   172в†’These tactics are disabled globally because they close goals that
   173в†’the player should learn to handle manually.
   174в†’-/
   175в†’
   176в†’/-- `trivial` attempts to close the goal using a combination of
   177в†’basic tactics (`rfl`, `exact True.intro`, `assumption`).
   178в†’
   179в†’Disabled because it bypasses the learning of specific proof steps.
   180в†’-/
   181в†’TacticDoc trivial
   182в†’
   183в†’/-- `decide` closes goals that are decidable propositions by
   184в†’evaluating them computationally.
   185в†’
   186в†’Disabled because it bypasses manual reasoning about finite types.
   187в†’-/
   188в†’TacticDoc В«decideВ»
   189в†’
   190в†’/-- `native_decide` is like `decide` but uses native code evaluation
   191в†’for faster computation.
   192в†’
   193в†’Disabled for the same reason as `decide`.
   194в†’-/
   195в†’TacticDoc native_decide
   196в†’
   197в†’/-- `simp` applies a set of simplification lemmas to the goal
   198в†’and hypotheses.
   199в†’
   200в†’Disabled in early levels to force manual rewriting and to ensure
   201в†’the player understands each proof step.
   202в†’-/
   203в†’TacticDoc simp
   204в†’
   205в†’/-- `aesop` is a general-purpose automation tactic that combines
   206в†’multiple strategies to close goals.
   207в†’
   208в†’Disabled to force manual proof construction.
   209в†’-/
   210в†’TacticDoc aesop
   211в†’
   212в†’/-- `simp_all` applies `simp` to all hypotheses and the goal
   213в†’simultaneously.
   214в†’
   215в†’Disabled for the same reason as `simp`.
   216в†’-/
   217в†’TacticDoc simp_all
   218в†’
   219в†’/-! ### MeetFin-specific disabled tactics and theorems -/
   220в†’
   221в†’/-- `fin_cases` automatically performs case analysis on elements of
   222в†’finite types like `Fin n`. It replaces a variable `x : Fin n` with
   223в†’each possible value `0, 1, ..., n-1`.
   224в†’
   225в†’Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
   226в†’-/
   227в†’TacticDoc fin_cases
   228в†’
   229в†’/-- `interval_cases` performs case analysis on a natural number or
   230в†’integer variable whose value is bounded by hypotheses.
   231в†’
   232в†’Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
   233в†’-/
   234в†’TacticDoc interval_cases
   235в†’
   236в†’/-- `Fin.forall_fin_two` states that a predicate holds for all
   237в†’elements of `Fin 2` if and only if it holds for `0` and `1`.
   238в†’-/
   239в†’TheoremDoc Fin.forall_fin_two as "Fin.forall_fin_two" in "Fin"
   240в†’
   241в†’/-- `Unique.eq_default` states that in a type with exactly one element,
   242в†’every element equals the default.
   243в†’-/
   244в†’TheoremDoc Unique.eq_default as "Unique.eq_default" in "Fin"
   245в†’
   246в†’/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
   247в†’any two elements are equal.
   248в†’-/
   249в†’TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"
   250в†’
   251в†’/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/
   252в†’
   253в†’/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
   254в†’any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
   255в†’`i.val < i.val + 1`.
   256в†’-/
   257в†’TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"
   258в†’
   259в†’/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
   260в†’This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
   261в†’-/
   262в†’TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"
   263в†’
   264в†’/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ в†” a = b` for
   265в†’elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
   266в†’-/
   267в†’TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"
   268в†’
   269в†’/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc в†” a = b`
   270в†’for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
   271в†’-/
   272в†’TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"
   273в†’
   274в†’/-! ### FinTuples-specific definitions and theorems -/
   275в†’
   276в†’/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_zero`.
   277в†’`Fin.cons a f 0 = a` вЂ” accessing the head of a cons'd tuple returns the prepended element. -/
   278в†’theorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (a : О±) (f : Fin n в†’ О±) :
   279в†’    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) в†’ О±) 0 = a := rfl
   280в†’
   281в†’/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_succ`.
   282в†’`Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` вЂ” accessing later elements of a cons'd tuple returns the tail. -/
   283в†’theorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (a : О±) (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (i : Fin n) :
   284в†’    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) в†’ О±) i.succ = f i := rfl
   285в†’
   286в†’/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
   287в†’producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.
   288в†’
   289в†’This is the non-dependent wrapper around `Fin.snoc` for use with
   290в†’concrete types like `Fin n в†’ в„•`.
   291в†’
   292в†’## Type
   293в†’`vecSnoc : (Fin n в†’ О±) в†’ О± в†’ Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±`
   294в†’
   295в†’## Element access
   296в†’- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` (the appended element)
   297в†’- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` (the original tuple)
   298в†’
   299в†’## Example
   300в†’`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
   301в†’-/
   302в†’def vecSnoc {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) : Fin (n + 1) в†’ О± :=
   303в†’  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => О±) f x
   304в†’
   305в†’/-- Accessing the last element of a snoc'd tuple gives back the appended element. -/
   306в†’theorem vecSnoc_last {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) :
   307в†’    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
   308в†’  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => О±) x f
   309в†’
   310в†’/-- Accessing an earlier element of a snoc'd tuple gives the original tuple's value. -/
   311в†’theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) (i : Fin n) :
   312в†’    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
   313в†’  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => О±) x f i
   314в†’
   315в†’/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.
   316в†’
   317в†’## Syntax
   318в†’```
   319в†’Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
   320в†’```
   321в†’
   322в†’## Type
   323в†’`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±) в†’ (Fin n в†’ О±)`
   324в†’
   325в†’## Definition
   326в†’`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`
   327в†’
   328в†’## Example
   329в†’`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.
   330в†’
   331в†’## Key identity (taught in the next level)
   332в†’`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` вЂ” every tuple equals
   333в†’its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
   334в†’-/
   335в†’DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"
   336в†’
   337в†’/-- Appending the last element to the init recovers the original tuple. -/
   338в†’theorem vecSnoc_self_init {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±) :
   339в†’    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f :=
   340в†’  Fin.snoc_init_self f
   341в†’
   342в†’/-- `vecSnoc_self_init f` states that
   343в†’`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.
   344в†’
   345в†’Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element.
   346в†’
   347в†’## Syntax
   348в†’```
   349в†’exact vecSnoc_self_init f  -- proves vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f
   350в†’```
   351в†’
   352в†’## When to use it
   353в†’When you need to decompose a tuple into its earlier elements
   354в†’and its last element.
   355в†’-/
   356в†’TheoremDoc vecSnoc_self_init as "vecSnoc_self_init" in "Fin"
   357в†’
   358в†’/-- `funext` is the function extensionality theorem: if two functions
   359в†’agree on every input, they are equal.
   360в†’
   361в†’Disabled in levels where the `ext` tactic should be used interactively.
   362в†’-/
   363в†’TheoremDoc funext as "funext" in "Fin"
   364в†’

import GameServer
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems

These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
-/

/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics

These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
about missing introductions.
-/

/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.

## Syntax
```
exact expression
```

## When to use it
When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
whose type matches the goal exactly.
-/
TacticDoc exact

/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.

## Syntax
```
rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
rw [в†ђ h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
```

## When to use it
When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P в†” Q` and want
to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
-/
TacticDoc rw

/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
antecedent of an implication into the context.

## Syntax
```
intro x       -- introduce one variable
intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `в€Ђ x, P x` or `P в†’ Q`.
-/
TacticDoc intro

/-- `cases x with | constructorв‚Ѓ => ... | constructorв‚‚ => ...`
performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.

## Syntax
```
cases x with
| constructorв‚Ѓ args => tacticв‚Ѓ
| constructorв‚‚ args => tacticв‚‚
```

## When to use it
When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `в€Ё` have `inl` and `inr`).
-/
TacticDoc cases

/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.

## Syntax
```
have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
```

## When to use it
When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
-/
TacticDoc В«haveВ»

/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `в€ѓ x, P x`.

## Syntax
```
use expr
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `в€ѓ x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
-/
TacticDoc use

/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P в€Ё Q`.

## Syntax
```
left
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P в€Ё Q` and you want to prove `P`.
-/
TacticDoc left

/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P в€Ё Q`.

## Syntax
```
right
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P в€Ё Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
-/
TacticDoc right

/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).

## Syntax
```
rfl
```

## When to use it
When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
-/
TacticDoc rfl

/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `в€§` or `в†”`)
into its components.

## Syntax
```
constructor
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P в€§ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P в†” Q`
(splits into `P в†’ Q` and `Q в†’ P`).
-/
TacticDoc constructor

/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
goals that can be decided by computation.

## Syntax
```
norm_num
```

## When to use it
When the goal is a concrete numerical fact like `2 + 3 = 5` or `7 < 10`.

Disabled in levels where manual arithmetic reasoning is the lesson.
-/
TacticDoc norm_num

/-! ### Baseline disabled tactics

These tactics are disabled globally because they close goals that
the player should learn to handle manually.
-/

/-- `trivial` attempts to close the goal using a combination of
basic tactics (`rfl`, `exact True.intro`, `assumption`).

Disabled because it bypasses the learning of specific proof steps.
-/
TacticDoc trivial

/-- `decide` closes goals that are decidable propositions by
evaluating them computationally.

Disabled because it bypasses manual reasoning about finite types.
-/
TacticDoc В«decideВ»

/-- `native_decide` is like `decide` but uses native code evaluation
for faster computation.

Disabled for the same reason as `decide`.
-/
TacticDoc native_decide

/-- `simp` applies a set of simplification lemmas to the goal
and hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual rewriting and to ensure
the player understands each proof step.
-/
TacticDoc simp

/-- `aesop` is a general-purpose automation tactic that combines
multiple strategies to close goals.

Disabled to force manual proof construction.
-/
TacticDoc aesop

/-- `simp_all` applies `simp` to all hypotheses and the goal
simultaneously.

Disabled for the same reason as `simp`.
-/
TacticDoc simp_all

/-! ### MeetFin-specific disabled tactics and theorems -/

/-- `fin_cases` automatically performs case analysis on elements of
finite types like `Fin n`. It replaces a variable `x : Fin n` with
each possible value `0, 1, ..., n-1`.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc fin_cases

/-- `interval_cases` performs case analysis on a natural number or
integer variable whose value is bounded by hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc interval_cases

/-- `Fin.forall_fin_two` states that a predicate holds for all
elements of `Fin 2` if and only if it holds for `0` and `1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.forall_fin_two as "Fin.forall_fin_two" in "Fin"

/-- `Unique.eq_default` states that in a type with exactly one element,
every element equals the default.
-/
TheoremDoc Unique.eq_default as "Unique.eq_default" in "Fin"

/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
any two elements are equal.
-/
TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
`i.val < i.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ в†” a = b` for
elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc в†” a = b`
for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinTuples-specific definitions and theorems -/

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_zero`.
`Fin.cons a f 0 = a` вЂ” accessing the head of a cons'd tuple returns the prepended element. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (a : О±) (f : Fin n в†’ О±) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) в†’ О±) 0 = a := rfl

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_succ`.
`Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` вЂ” accessing later elements of a cons'd tuple returns the tail. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (a : О±) (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) в†’ О±) i.succ = f i := rfl

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

This is the non-dependent wrapper around `Fin.snoc` for use with
concrete types like `Fin n в†’ в„•`.

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n в†’ О±) в†’ О± в†’ Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` (the appended element)
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` (the original tuple)

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
def vecSnoc {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) : Fin (n + 1) в†’ О± :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => О±) f x

/-- Accessing the last element of a snoc'd tuple gives back the appended element. -/
theorem vecSnoc_last {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) :
    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => О±) x f

/-- Accessing an earlier element of a snoc'd tuple gives the original tuple's value. -/
theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin n в†’ О±) (x : О±) (i : Fin n) :
    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => О±) x f i

/-- `Fin.init f` drops the last element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.init f    -- drop the last element
```

## Type
`Fin.init : (Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±) в†’ (Fin n в†’ О±)`

## Definition
`Fin.init f i = f i.castSucc`

## Example
`Fin.init ![10, 20, 30]` has values `10` at index 0 and `20` at index 1.

## Key identity (taught in the next level)
`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f` вЂ” every tuple equals
its init snoc'd with its last element (`vecSnoc_self_init`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.init as "Fin.init"

/-- Appending the last element to the init recovers the original tuple. -/
theorem vecSnoc_self_init {n : в„•} {О± : Type*} (f : Fin (n + 1) в†’ О±) :
    vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f :=
  Fin.snoc_init_self f

/-- `vecSnoc_self_init f` states that
`vecSnoc (Fin.init f) (f (Fin.last n)) = f`.

Every tuple equals its init snoc'd with its last element.

## Syntax
```
exact vecSnoc_self_init f  -- proves vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its earlier elements
and its last element.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_self_init as "vecSnoc_self_init" in "Fin"

/-- `funext` is the function extensionality theorem: if two functions
agree on every input, they are equal.

Disabled in levels where the `ext` tactic should be used interactively.
-/
TheoremDoc funext as "funext" in "Fin"

    21в†’natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` вЂ” the type checker
    22в†’can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 1

Title "What is Fin n?"

Introduction "
# What is `Fin n`?

In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ вЂ” the first $n$ natural numbers.

In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.

More precisely:
$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$

**Why not just use `в„•`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` вЂ” the type checker
can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.

This is an example of a *subtype* вЂ” a type carved out of a larger type
by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : в„• // k < n }`.

To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
`вџЁk, proofвџ©`, where:
- `k` is the natural number
- `proof` shows `k < n`

For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
goals like `3 < 5`.

**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Try `exact вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©`.

*Notation*: type `вџЁ` as `\\langle` and `вџ©` as `\\rangle`.
"

/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
Statement : Fin 5 := by
  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `вџЁk, by omegaвџ©` where `k` is any
  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©`."
  exact вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©

Conclusion "
Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
`Fin 5`.

The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** вЂ” a
number and a proof that it's in range. The `вџЁk, by omegaвџ©` idiom
will appear throughout this world.

In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
That's what the second component does.

*Note*: `exact 3` also works here because Lean can automatically coerce
a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known. But in later
worlds where the bound is a variable, you'll need the explicit
constructor `вџЁk, proofвџ©`.
"

/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `в„•` and `в„¤`.
It handles equalities (`=`), inequalities (`<`, `в‰¤`, `в‰Ґ`, `>`),
and negated equalities (`в‰ `).

## Syntax
```
omega
```

## When to use it
- When the goal follows from linear arithmetic: `3 < 5`, `n + 1 в‰¤ m`
- When hypotheses contradict each other: if you have `h1 : x = 5` and
  `h2 : x < 5`, omega sees the contradiction and closes any goal
- When multiple hypotheses combine: omega reasons across ALL hypotheses
  simultaneously, not just one at a time

## Examples
```
-- Goal: 3 < 5
omega

-- h1 : x = 5, h2 : x < 5 в†’ closes any goal (contradiction)
omega

-- h1 : a в‰¤ b, h2 : b < c в†’ Goal: a < c
omega
```

## Warning
`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
-/
TacticDoc omega

/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.

An element of `Fin n` consists of:
- `val : в„•` вЂ” the underlying natural number
- `isLt : val < n` вЂ” a proof that the number is in range

`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : в„• // k < n }`.

## Construction
Use the anonymous constructor: `вџЁk, proofвџ©`

## Example
`вџЁ3, by omegaвџ© : Fin 5` вЂ” the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.

## Warning
`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
`Fin 0` is **empty** вЂ” there is no natural number less than 0.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"

/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.

Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `вџЁval, isLtвџ©`.

## Example
`вџЁ3, by omegaвџ© : Fin 5`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"

NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases В«haveВ» use left right rfl constructor
NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

1в†’import Game.Metadata
     2в†’
     3в†’World "MeetFin"
     4в†’Level 2
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "The Number Inside"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Extracting the Natural Number
    10в†’
    11в†’Every element `i : Fin n` carries two pieces of data:
    12в†’- `i.val : в„•` вЂ” the underlying natural number
    13в†’- `i.isLt : i.val < n` вЂ” the proof that it's in range
    14в†’
    15в†’The field `.val` lets you **extract** the natural number from a `Fin`
    16в†’element. The field `.isLt` gives you the **bound proof** directly.
    17в†’
    18в†’Lean also has a *coercion* notation: when a natural number is expected,
    19в†’you can write `(i : в„•)` or `в†‘i` (typed `\\uparrow i`), and Lean
    20в†’automatically inserts `.val`. So `(i : в„•)`, `в†‘i`, and `i.val` all
    21в†’mean the same thing.
    22в†’
    23в†’Look at the goal: it says `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3`. The hypothesis `h` says
    24в†’`i.val = 3`. Since `в†‘i` IS `i.val`, the hypothesis `h` matches the
    25в†’goal exactly.
    26в†’
    27в†’**Your task**: Use `exact h` to close the goal.
    28в†’"
    29в†’
    30в†’/-- The coercion `в†‘i` is the same as `i.val`. -/
    31в†’Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : (i : в„•) = 3 := by
    32в†’  Hint "The goal `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3` is the same as `i.val = 3`. The
    33в†’  hypothesis `{h}` says exactly `i.val = 3`. Try `exact h`."
    34в†’  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h` works because `(i : в„•)` and `i.val`
    35в†’  are definitionally the same вЂ” Lean sees no difference."
    36в†’  exact h
    37в†’
    38в†’Conclusion "
    39в†’You've just seen that `в†‘i`, `(i : в„•)`, and `i.val` are all the same
    40в†’thing вЂ” three notations for extracting the natural number from a `Fin`
    41в†’element.
    42в†’
    43в†’| Notation | Meaning |
    44в†’|---|---|
    45в†’| `i.val` | Dot notation for the value field |
    46в†’| `в†‘i` | Coercion arrow (typed `\\uparrow`) |
    47в†’| `(i : в„•)` | Type ascription coercion |
    48в†’
    49в†’All three are **definitionally equal** in Lean вЂ” the type checker
    50в†’treats them as identical without any proof needed.
    51в†’
    52в†’The other field, `i.isLt`, gives you a proof that `i.val < n`. You'll
    53в†’use `.isLt` in the next level to prove a bound.
    54в†’"
    55в†’
    56в†’/-- `Fin.val` extracts the underlying natural number from a `Fin n`
    57в†’element. If `i : Fin n`, then `i.val : в„•` satisfies `i.val < n`.
    58в†’
    59в†’The coercion `в†‘i` (typed `\\uparrow i`) is notation for `i.val`
    60в†’when a natural number is expected.
    61в†’
    62в†’## Example
    63в†’If `i : Fin 5` and `i.val = 3`, then:
    64в†’- `i.val = 3`
    65в†’- `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3`
    66в†’
    67в†’## Related
    68в†’`i.isLt` gives a proof that `i.val < n`.
    69в†’-/
    70в†’DefinitionDoc Fin.val as "Fin.val"
    71в†’
    72в†’NewDefinition Fin.val
    73в†’
    74в†’DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
    75в†’

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 2

Title "The Number Inside"

Introduction "
# Extracting the Natural Number

Every element `i : Fin n` carries two pieces of data:
- `i.val : в„•` вЂ” the underlying natural number
- `i.isLt : i.val < n` вЂ” the proof that it's in range

The field `.val` lets you **extract** the natural number from a `Fin`
element. The field `.isLt` gives you the **bound proof** directly.

Lean also has a *coercion* notation: when a natural number is expected,
you can write `(i : в„•)` or `в†‘i` (typed `\\uparrow i`), and Lean
automatically inserts `.val`. So `(i : в„•)`, `в†‘i`, and `i.val` all
mean the same thing.

Look at the goal: it says `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3`. The hypothesis `h` says
`i.val = 3`. Since `в†‘i` IS `i.val`, the hypothesis `h` matches the
goal exactly.

**Your task**: Use `exact h` to close the goal.
"

/-- The coercion `в†‘i` is the same as `i.val`. -/
Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : (i : в„•) = 3 := by
  Hint "The goal `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3` is the same as `i.val = 3`. The
  hypothesis `{h}` says exactly `i.val = 3`. Try `exact h`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h` works because `(i : в„•)` and `i.val`
  are definitionally the same вЂ” Lean sees no difference."
  exact h

Conclusion "
You've just seen that `в†‘i`, `(i : в„•)`, and `i.val` are all the same
thing вЂ” three notations for extracting the natural number from a `Fin`
element.

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `i.val` | Dot notation for the value field |
| `в†‘i` | Coercion arrow (typed `\\uparrow`) |
| `(i : в„•)` | Type ascription coercion |

All three are **definitionally equal** in Lean вЂ” the type checker
treats them as identical without any proof needed.

The other field, `i.isLt`, gives you a proof that `i.val < n`. You'll
use `.isLt` in the next level to prove a bound.
"

/-- `Fin.val` extracts the underlying natural number from a `Fin n`
element. If `i : Fin n`, then `i.val : в„•` satisfies `i.val < n`.

The coercion `в†‘i` (typed `\\uparrow i`) is notation for `i.val`
when a natural number is expected.

## Example
If `i : Fin 5` and `i.val = 3`, then:
- `i.val = 3`
- `(в†‘i : в„•) = 3`

## Related
`i.isLt` gives a proof that `i.val < n`.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.val as "Fin.val"

NewDefinition Fin.val

1в†’import Game.Metadata
     2в†’
     3в†’World "MeetFin"
     4в†’Level 3
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "The Upper Bound"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Fin 5 Does Not Contain 5
    10в†’
    11в†’The most common misconception about `Fin n` is confusing
    12в†’$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ with $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. The elements
    13в†’of `Fin 5` are $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ вЂ” **not** $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\\}$.
    14в†’
    15в†’Let's make this concrete: no element of `Fin 5` has value `5`.
    16в†’
    17в†’**Strategy**: Introduce `x` and assume `h : x.val = 5` for
    18в†’contradiction. Then use `x.isLt` (which says `x.val < 5`) to get a
    19в†’contradiction with `h` via `omega`.
    20в†’
    21в†’Step by step:
    22в†’1. `intro x` вЂ” introduces the element
    23в†’2. `intro h` вЂ” assumes `x.val = 5` (since `в‰ ` means `= в†’ False`)
    24в†’3. `have hlt := x.isLt` вЂ” extracts the bound `x.val < 5`
    25в†’4. `omega` вЂ” sees `x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5` are contradictory
    26в†’"
    27в†’
    28в†’/-- No element of `Fin 5` has value `5`. -/
    29в†’Statement : в€Ђ x : Fin 5, x.val в‰  5 := by

    31в†’  to assume `x.val = 5` for contradiction."
    32в†’  intro x
    33в†’  intro h
    34в†’  Hint "Now extract the bound proof: `have hlt := x.isLt`. This gives

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 3

Title "The Upper Bound"

Introduction "
# Fin 5 Does Not Contain 5

The most common misconception about `Fin n` is confusing
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ with $\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. The elements
of `Fin 5` are $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ вЂ” **not** $\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\\}$.

Let's make this concrete: no element of `Fin 5` has value `5`.

**Strategy**: Introduce `x` and assume `h : x.val = 5` for
contradiction. Then use `x.isLt` (which says `x.val < 5`) to get a
contradiction with `h` via `omega`.

Step by step:
1. `intro x` вЂ” introduces the element
2. `intro h` вЂ” assumes `x.val = 5` (since `в‰ ` means `= в†’ False`)
3. `have hlt := x.isLt` вЂ” extracts the bound `x.val < 5`
4. `omega` вЂ” sees `x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5` are contradictory
"

/-- No element of `Fin 5` has value `5`. -/
Statement : в€Ђ x : Fin 5, x.val в‰  5 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro x` to introduce the element, then `intro h`
  to assume `x.val = 5` for contradiction."
  intro x
  intro h
  Hint "Now extract the bound proof: `have hlt := x.isLt`. This gives
  `hlt : x.val < 5`."
  have hlt := x.isLt
  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `x.val = 5` and `{hlt}` saying
  `x.val < 5`. These contradict each other вЂ” `omega` sees this."
  omega

Conclusion "
You've now proved what the introduction warned about: `Fin 5` contains
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ and **not** 5.

The key ingredient was `.isLt`: every `x : Fin n` carries a proof
that `x.val < n`. Combined with the assumption `x.val = n`, you get
a contradiction that `omega` resolves.

Notice that `omega` is doing something new here: in Level 1, it proved
a simple fact (`3 < 5`). Here, it **derives a contradiction** from two
hypotheses (`x.val = 5` and `x.val < 5`). `omega` reasons across all
hypotheses simultaneously вЂ” it sees everything in your context that
involves linear arithmetic over `в„•` and `в„¤`, and either proves the
goal or finds a contradiction. This makes it your go-to tactic for
any arithmetic reasoning in this course.

This pattern вЂ” *extract the bound, derive a contradiction* вЂ” appears
whenever someone (or some proof branch) tries to use an out-of-range
value. The bound proof `.isLt` is the safety net that the type system
provides.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# The Round-Trip
    10в†’
    11в†’In Level 1, you *constructed* a `Fin` element with `вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©`.
    12в†’In Level 2, you *extracted* its value with `.val`.
    13в†’

    15в†’its value? You get back the number you put in:
    16в†’
    17в†’$$\\langle 3, \\text{proof} \\rangle.\\text{val} = 3$$
    18в†’

    20в†’works. In Lean, this means the proof is just `rfl` (reflexivity).
    21в†’

    23в†’checker itself sees that `вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©.val` *computes* to `3`.
    24в†’
    25в†’**Your task**: Prove this round-trip property with `rfl`.
    26в†’"
    27в†’
    28в†’/-- The value of an explicitly constructed Fin element is the number you put in. -/
    29в†’Statement : (вџЁ3, by omegaвџ© : Fin 5).val = 3 := by
    30в†’  Hint "This is true by definition. Try `rfl`."

    32в†’

    35в†’**definitional equality**: the type checker reduces `вџЁ3, _вџ©.val` to
    36в†’`3` automatically.
    37в†’
    38в†’This round-trip property (`вџЁk, _вџ©.val = k`) is an instance of a
    39в†’general pattern called *beta reduction*: constructing a value and then
    40в†’projecting out a field gives back the original data.
    41в†’

    43в†’a type bundles data with a proof, constructing then extracting gives
    44в†’back the data.
    45в†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 4

Title "Construction and Extraction"

Introduction "
# The Round-Trip

In Level 1, you *constructed* a `Fin` element with `вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©`.
In Level 2, you *extracted* its value with `.val`.

What happens when you construct an element and immediately extract
its value? You get back the number you put in:

$$\\langle 3, \\text{proof} \\rangle.\\text{val} = 3$$

This equality holds **by definition** вЂ” it's built into how `Fin`
works. In Lean, this means the proof is just `rfl` (reflexivity).

No rewriting, no arithmetic, no tactics beyond `rfl`. The type
checker itself sees that `вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©.val` *computes* to `3`.

**Your task**: Prove this round-trip property with `rfl`.
"

/-- The value of an explicitly constructed Fin element is the number you put in. -/
Statement : (вџЁ3, by omegaвџ© : Fin 5).val = 3 := by
  Hint "This is true by definition. Try `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
The proof is `rfl` вЂ” no computation or reasoning needed. This is
**definitional equality**: the type checker reduces `вџЁ3, _вџ©.val` to
`3` automatically.

This round-trip property (`вџЁk, _вџ©.val = k`) is an instance of a
general pattern called *beta reduction*: constructing a value and then
projecting out a field gives back the original data.

You'll see this principle again with other subtypes in Lean. Whenever
a type bundles data with a proof, constructing then extracting gives
back the data.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

1в†’import Game.Metadata

     4в†’Level 5
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "When Are Fin Elements Equal?"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Fin Extensionality
    10в†’
    11в†’When are two elements of `Fin n` equal? Exactly when their underlying
    12в†’natural number values are equal.
    13в†’
    14в†’This is stated by `Fin.ext_iff`:
    15в†’
    16в†’$$i = j \\iff i.\\text{val} = j.\\text{val}$$
    17в†’
    18в†’You can use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to convert a goal about `Fin` equality
    19в†’into a goal about natural number equality. This is one of the most
    20в†’important moves in `Fin` reasoning.
    21в†’
    22в†’**Your task**: Given two elements `i j : Fin 5` with `h : i.val = j.val`,
    23в†’prove that `i = j`.
    24в†’"
    25в†’
    26в†’/-- Two `Fin 5` elements with the same value are equal. -/
    27в†’Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i.val = j.val) : i = j := by
    28в†’  Hint "The goal is `i = j` where `i j : Fin 5`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`
    29в†’  to convert this to `i.val = j.val`."
    30в†’  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    31в†’  Hint "Now the goal is `i.val = j.val`, which is exactly `{h}`."
    32в†’  exact h
    33в†’
    34в†’Conclusion "
    35в†’The pattern `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` converts `Fin` equality to value equality.
    36в†’You'll use this pattern repeatedly вЂ” whenever you need to prove two `Fin`
    37в†’elements are equal, reduce it to their values.
    38в†’
    39в†’The converse direction is trivial: if `i = j` then certainly
    40в†’`i.val = j.val` (applying a function preserves equality). You'll
    41в†’practice this direction in the next level.
    42в†’
    43в†’In plain mathematics, this is the obvious fact that elements of
    44в†’$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ are just numbers, and two numbers are equal
    45в†’when they're the same number. In Lean, `Fin n` is a *structured type*,
    46в†’so you need `ext_iff` to access this.
    47в†’
    48в†’*Shortcut*: the `ext` tactic does the same thing as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`
    49в†’вЂ” it converts `i = j` to `i.val = j.val` directly. You'll see `ext`
    50в†’used for other types later (like function extensionality). We'll stick
    51в†’with `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for now since it makes the step explicit.
    52в†’"
    53в†’
    54в†’/-- `Fin.ext_iff` states that two elements of `Fin n` are equal if and
    55в†’only if their underlying values are equal:
    56в†’
    57в†’`a = b в†” a.val = b.val`
    58в†’
    59в†’## Syntax
    60в†’```
    61в†’rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    62в†’```
    63в†’Converts a goal `a = b` (for `a b : Fin n`) to `a.val = b.val`.
    64в†’
    65в†’```
    66в†’rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
    67в†’```
    68в†’Converts a hypothesis `h : a = b` to `h : a.val = b.val`.
    69в†’
    70в†’## When to use it
    71в†’Whenever you need to prove or decompose equality of `Fin` elements.
    72в†’
    73в†’## Example
    74в†’```
    75в†’-- Goal: i = j  (where i j : Fin 5)
    76в†’rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    77в†’-- Goal: i.val = j.val
    78в†’```
    79в†’-/

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 5

Title "When Are Fin Elements Equal?"

Introduction "
# Fin Extensionality

When are two elements of `Fin n` equal? Exactly when their underlying
natural number values are equal.

This is stated by `Fin.ext_iff`:

$$i = j \\iff i.\\text{val} = j.\\text{val}$$

You can use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to convert a goal about `Fin` equality
into a goal about natural number equality. This is one of the most
important moves in `Fin` reasoning.

**Your task**: Given two elements `i j : Fin 5` with `h : i.val = j.val`,
prove that `i = j`.
"

/-- Two `Fin 5` elements with the same value are equal. -/
Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i.val = j.val) : i = j := by
  Hint "The goal is `i = j` where `i j : Fin 5`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`
  to convert this to `i.val = j.val`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
  Hint "Now the goal is `i.val = j.val`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
The pattern `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` converts `Fin` equality to value equality.
You'll use this pattern repeatedly вЂ” whenever you need to prove two `Fin`
elements are equal, reduce it to their values.

The converse direction is trivial: if `i = j` then certainly
`i.val = j.val` (applying a function preserves equality). You'll
practice this direction in the next level.

In plain mathematics, this is the obvious fact that elements of
$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$ are just numbers, and two numbers are equal
when they're the same number. In Lean, `Fin n` is a *structured type*,
so you need `ext_iff` to access this.

*Shortcut*: the `ext` tactic does the same thing as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`
вЂ” it converts `i = j` to `i.val = j.val` directly. You'll see `ext`
used for other types later (like function extensionality). We'll stick
with `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for now since it makes the step explicit.
"

/-- `Fin.ext_iff` states that two elements of `Fin n` are equal if and
only if their underlying values are equal:

`a = b в†” a.val = b.val`

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.ext_iff]
```
Converts a goal `a = b` (for `a b : Fin n`) to `a.val = b.val`.

```
rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
```
Converts a hypothesis `h : a = b` to `h : a.val = b.val`.

## When to use it
Whenever you need to prove or decompose equality of `Fin` elements.

## Example
```
-- Goal: i = j  (where i j : Fin 5)
rw [Fin.ext_iff]
-- Goal: i.val = j.val
```
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.ext_iff as "Fin.ext_iff" in "Fin"

TheoremTab "Fin"
NewTheorem Fin.ext_iff

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Fin Equality Implies Value Equality
    10в†’
    11в†’In Level 5, you proved that equal values imply equal `Fin` elements
    12в†’(the backward direction of `Fin.ext_iff`).
    13в†’
    14в†’The converse is also true: if two `Fin` elements are equal, then
    15в†’their values are equal. This follows from a basic principle: applying
    16в†’a function to both sides of an equality preserves the equality. Since
    17в†’`.val` is a function from `Fin n` to `в„•`, if `i = j` then
    18в†’`i.val = j.val`.
    19в†’
    20в†’In Lean, the simplest proof uses `rw` directly. If `h : i = j`, then
    21в†’`rw [h]` replaces `i` with `j` in the goal, turning `i.val = j.val`
    22в†’into `j.val = j.val` вЂ” which closes by reflexivity.
    23в†’
    24в†’**Your task**: Prove that `Fin` equality implies value equality.
    25в†’"
    26в†’
    27в†’/-- If two `Fin` elements are equal, their values are equal. -/
    28в†’Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i = j) : i.val = j.val := by
    29в†’  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `i = j`. Use `rw [h]` to replace `i`
    30в†’  with `j` in the goal."
    31в†’  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [h]` turns the goal into `j.val = j.val`,
    32в†’  which Lean closes automatically."
    33в†’  rw [h]
    34в†’
    35в†’Conclusion "
    36в†’Both directions of `Fin.ext_iff` are now concrete:
    37в†’
    38в†’| Direction | Statement | Proof |
    39в†’|---|---|---|
    40в†’| Values в†’ Elements | `i.val = j.val в†’ i = j` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; exact h` |
    41в†’| Elements в†’ Values | `i = j в†’ i.val = j.val` | `rw [h]` |
    42в†’
    43в†’Together, they say: **`Fin` equality is completely determined by
    44в†’value equality.** The type-level wrapper carries no extra
    45в†’information beyond the natural number inside.
    46в†’
    47в†’In the next level, you'll learn to rewrite hypotheses (not just the
    48в†’goal) using `rw [...] at h`.
    49в†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 6

Title "The Converse Direction"

Introduction "
# Fin Equality Implies Value Equality

In Level 5, you proved that equal values imply equal `Fin` elements
(the backward direction of `Fin.ext_iff`).

The converse is also true: if two `Fin` elements are equal, then
their values are equal. This follows from a basic principle: applying
a function to both sides of an equality preserves the equality. Since
`.val` is a function from `Fin n` to `в„•`, if `i = j` then
`i.val = j.val`.

In Lean, the simplest proof uses `rw` directly. If `h : i = j`, then
`rw [h]` replaces `i` with `j` in the goal, turning `i.val = j.val`
into `j.val = j.val` вЂ” which closes by reflexivity.

**Your task**: Prove that `Fin` equality implies value equality.
"

/-- If two `Fin` elements are equal, their values are equal. -/
Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i = j) : i.val = j.val := by
  Hint "You have `{h}` saying `i = j`. Use `rw [h]` to replace `i`
  with `j` in the goal."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [h]` turns the goal into `j.val = j.val`,
  which Lean closes automatically."
  rw [h]

Conclusion "
Both directions of `Fin.ext_iff` are now concrete:

| Direction | Statement | Proof |
|---|---|---|
| Values в†’ Elements | `i.val = j.val в†’ i = j` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; exact h` |
| Elements в†’ Values | `i = j в†’ i.val = j.val` | `rw [h]` |

Together, they say: **`Fin` equality is completely determined by
value equality.** The type-level wrapper carries no extra
information beyond the natural number inside.

In the next level, you'll learn to rewrite hypotheses (not just the
goal) using `rw [...] at h`.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

    23в†’then `omega` can combine `h` and `p` to close the goal.

    25в†’

    28в†’  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` to convert `{h}` from

    32в†’  `omega` can combine these to conclude `j.val < 3`."
    33в†’  omega
    34в†’
    35в†’Conclusion "
    36в†’You now know both directions of `Fin.ext_iff`:
    37в†’

    41в†’| Hypothesis: `h : i = j` в†’ `h : i.val = j.val` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | Extracts value equality from Fin equality |
    42в†’

    44в†’Whenever you want to rewrite a hypothesis instead of the goal,
    45в†’add `at h` (where `h` is the hypothesis name).
    46в†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 7

Title "Rewriting Hypotheses"

Introduction "
# Rewriting Hypotheses with `at`

In Level 5, you used `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to rewrite the **goal**.
But `rw` can also rewrite **hypotheses**. The syntax is:
```
rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
```
This converts a hypothesis `h : i = j` into `h : i.val = j.val`.

Here you have `h : i = j` (a `Fin` equality) and `p : i.val < 3`.
The goal asks for `j.val < 3`. Notice that `p` mentions `i.val`
but the goal mentions `j.val`.

Strategy: use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` to extract the value equality,
then `omega` can combine `h` and `p` to close the goal.
"

/-- Transfer a bound through a Fin equality. -/
Statement (i j : Fin 5) (h : i = j) (p : i.val < 3) : j.val < 3 := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` to convert `{h}` from
  a `Fin` equality to a value equality `i.val = j.val`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `i.val = j.val` and `{p}` says `i.val < 3`.
  `omega` can combine these to conclude `j.val < 3`."
  omega

Conclusion "
You now know both directions of `Fin.ext_iff`:

| Direction | Syntax | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Goal: `i = j` в†’ `i.val = j.val` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` | Reduces Fin equality to value equality |
| Hypothesis: `h : i = j` в†’ `h : i.val = j.val` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | Extracts value equality from Fin equality |

The `at h` modifier works with any `rw` вЂ” not just `Fin.ext_iff`.
Whenever you want to rewrite a hypothesis instead of the goal,
add `at h` (where `h` is the hypothesis name).
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

1в†’import Game.Metadata

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 8

Title "Transferring Bounds"

Introduction "
# Rewriting Through Fin Equality

In Level 7, you used `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` to convert a Fin
equality into a value equality, then closed with `omega`.

There's a more direct approach. Since `h : a = b` is an equality
of `Fin` elements, you can use `rw [h] at p` to replace `a` with
`b` directly in any hypothesis `p`. This turns
`p : a.val в‰¤ 2` into `p : b.val в‰¤ 2` вЂ” exactly the goal.

This is different from Level 7's technique:
- Level 7: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` вЂ” converts the *equality* from
  Fin-level to value-level, then uses `omega`
- This level: `rw [h] at p` вЂ” rewrites a *different hypothesis*
  using the Fin equality directly

Both are useful. The first decomposes the equality; the second
propagates it.

**Your task**: Transfer a bound from `a` to `b`.
"

/-- Transfer a bound through a Fin equality by direct rewriting. -/
Statement (a b : Fin 5) (h : a = b) (p : a.val в‰¤ 2) : b.val в‰¤ 2 := by
  Hint "Use `rw [h] at p` to replace `a` with `b` in `{p}`.
  Since `{h}` says `a = b`, this turns `{p}` into `b.val в‰¤ 2`."
  rw [h] at p
  Hint "Now `{p}` says `b.val в‰¤ 2`, which is exactly the goal."
  exact p

Conclusion "
You've now seen two ways to use a Fin equality hypothesis:

| Technique | Syntax | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Decompose the equality | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | Converts `h : a = b` to `h : a.val = b.val` |
| Propagate the equality | `rw [h] at p` | Replaces `a` with `b` in hypothesis `p` |

The first technique (Level 7) is useful when you need the *value*
equality for arithmetic. The second (this level) is useful when you
want to directly transform another hypothesis.

Both are instances of a general principle: `rw [...] at h` can
rewrite any hypothesis, not just the goal.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 9

Title "The Round-Trip Property"

Introduction "
# Taking Apart and Reassembling

In Level 1, you *constructed* a `Fin` element with `вџЁk, by omegaвџ©`.
In Level 2, you *extracted* its parts with `.val` and `.isLt`.

A natural question: if you know an element's value, can you identify
it with an explicit `вџЁk, proofвџ©` term?

Here you have `i : Fin 5` and a hypothesis `h : i.val = 3`. The
goal asks you to show `i = вџЁ3, by omegaвџ©` вЂ” that `i` is the
element with value 3.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality
to a value equality, then use `h` to close.
"

/-- If a Fin element has value 3, it equals the explicit construction вџЁ3, _вџ©. -/
Statement (i : Fin 5) (h : i.val = 3) : i = вџЁ3, by omegaвџ© := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to convert the `Fin` equality to a
  value equality."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
  Hint "The goal is now `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
The round-trip principle: if you know what value a `Fin` element
carries, you can identify it with the explicit construction.

This pattern has a name in Lean's library: **`Fin.eta`**. It says
that any `Fin` element `i` equals `вџЁi.val, i.isLtвџ©` вЂ” its own
reconstruction from components. More generally, this is an instance
of **`Subtype.eta`**: for any subtype `{ x : О± // P x }`, an element
equals its reconstruction from `.val` and the proof. You'll see this
eta principle again whenever you work with subtypes.

This is `Fin.ext_iff` in action вЂ” equality of `Fin` elements reduces
to equality of their values, and once the values match, the elements
are the same.
"

     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Proof Irrelevance for Fin
    10в†’
    11в†’A `Fin n` element bundles a value with a *proof* that the value is
    12в†’in range. But what if two elements have the same value? Are they
    13в†’the same element?
    14в†’
    15в†’The answer: **only the value matters**. Two `Fin` elements with the
    16в†’same underlying natural number are equal, regardless of how their
    17в†’bound proofs were obtained. This follows directly from `Fin.ext_iff`:
    18в†’equality depends only on `.val`.
    19в†’
    20в†’Here you have two elements `a` and `b` of `Fin 5`, and you know
    21в†’`ha : a.val = 3` and `hb : b.val = 3`. Since both have value `3`,
    22в†’they must be equal.
    23в†’
    24в†’In practice, you might get bound proofs from different sources вЂ”
    25в†’one from a hypothesis, one from `omega`, one from a lemma. Proof
    26в†’irrelevance means you never need to worry about *which* proof was
    27в†’used.
    28в†’
    29в†’**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce to a value equality,
    30в†’then use `omega` to close the arithmetic (since `ha` and `hb`
    31в†’together imply `a.val = b.val`).
    32в†’"
    33в†’
    34в†’/-- Fin elements with the same value are equal, regardless of the bound proof. -/
    35в†’Statement (a b : Fin 5) (ha : a.val = 3) (hb : b.val = 3) : a = b := by
    36в†’  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality to a
    37в†’  value equality."
    38в†’  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
    39в†’  Hint "Now the goal is `a.val = b.val`. You know `{ha}` and
    40в†’  `{hb}` вЂ” both values are `3`. `omega` can close this."
    41в†’  omega
    42в†’
    43в†’Conclusion "
    44в†’This property is called **proof irrelevance**: the identity of a
    45в†’`Fin` element depends only on its value, not on which proof
    46в†’witnesses the bound. This is a direct consequence of the eta
    47в†’principle (`Fin.eta` / `Subtype.eta` from Level 9): since an
    48в†’element is determined by its value, two elements with the same
    49в†’value must be equal вЂ” regardless of how their bound proofs were
    50в†’obtained.
    51в†’
    52в†’Notice the proof pattern: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` then `omega`. This
    53в†’two-step combo вЂ” **reduce to values, then compute** вЂ” is the
    54в†’standard way to prove `Fin` equalities when the values can be
    55в†’determined from hypotheses. We'll call this the **Fin equality
    56в†’pattern**: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`. You'll use it frequently.
    57в†’
    58в†’The deeper reason this works is that **`Prop` is proof-irrelevant
    59в†’in Lean**: any two proofs of the same proposition are considered
    60в†’equal. So `hv1 : k < n` and `hv2 : k < n` вЂ” no matter how they
    61в†’were obtained вЂ” are the same proof. This means `вџЁk, hv1вџ© = вџЁk, hv2вџ©`
    62в†’automatically, and equality of `Fin` elements reduces to equality
    63в†’of values.
    64в†’
    65в†’This isn't special to `Fin` вЂ” it applies to **every subtype** in Lean.
    66в†’Any type of the form `{ x : О± // P x }` (a value bundled with a
    67в†’proof) has the same property: two elements are equal when their values
    68в†’are equal, regardless of the proofs. The same `ext_iff + omega`
    69в†’pattern works for any subtype where the predicate involves arithmetic.
    70в†’You'll meet other subtypes later (like `{ n : в„• // n.Prime }`), and

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 10

Title "Only the Value Matters"

Introduction "
# Proof Irrelevance for Fin

A `Fin n` element bundles a value with a *proof* that the value is
in range. But what if two elements have the same value? Are they
the same element?

The answer: **only the value matters**. Two `Fin` elements with the
same underlying natural number are equal, regardless of how their
bound proofs were obtained. This follows directly from `Fin.ext_iff`:
equality depends only on `.val`.

Here you have two elements `a` and `b` of `Fin 5`, and you know
`ha : a.val = 3` and `hb : b.val = 3`. Since both have value `3`,
they must be equal.

In practice, you might get bound proofs from different sources вЂ”
one from a hypothesis, one from `omega`, one from a lemma. Proof
irrelevance means you never need to worry about *which* proof was
used.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce to a value equality,
then use `omega` to close the arithmetic (since `ha` and `hb`
together imply `a.val = b.val`).
"

/-- Fin elements with the same value are equal, regardless of the bound proof. -/
Statement (a b : Fin 5) (ha : a.val = 3) (hb : b.val = 3) : a = b := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` to reduce the `Fin` equality to a
  value equality."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff]
  Hint "Now the goal is `a.val = b.val`. You know `{ha}` and
  `{hb}` вЂ” both values are `3`. `omega` can close this."
  omega

Conclusion "
This property is called **proof irrelevance**: the identity of a
`Fin` element depends only on its value, not on which proof
witnesses the bound. This is a direct consequence of the eta
principle (`Fin.eta` / `Subtype.eta` from Level 9): since an
element is determined by its value, two elements with the same
value must be equal вЂ” regardless of how their bound proofs were
obtained.

Notice the proof pattern: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` then `omega`. This
two-step combo вЂ” **reduce to values, then compute** вЂ” is the
standard way to prove `Fin` equalities when the values can be
determined from hypotheses. We'll call this the **Fin equality
pattern**: `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`. You'll use it frequently.

The deeper reason this works is that **`Prop` is proof-irrelevant
in Lean**: any two proofs of the same proposition are considered
equal. So `hv1 : k < n` and `hv2 : k < n` вЂ” no matter how they
were obtained вЂ” are the same proof. This means `вџЁk, hv1вџ© = вџЁk, hv2вџ©`
automatically, and equality of `Fin` elements reduces to equality
of values.

This isn't special to `Fin` вЂ” it applies to **every subtype** in Lean.
Any type of the form `{ x : О± // P x }` (a value bundled with a
proof) has the same property: two elements are equal when their values
are equal, regardless of the proofs. The same `ext_iff + omega`
pattern works for any subtype where the predicate involves arithmetic.
You'll meet other subtypes later (like `{ n : в„• // n.Prime }`), and
proof irrelevance will apply there too.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# From Fin 5 to Fin (n + 1)
    10в†’
    11в†’Every level so far has used a *concrete* type вЂ” `Fin 5`, `Fin 3`,
    12в†’`Fin 2`. But the techniques you've learned work for *any* `n`.
    13в†’
    14в†’Here you'll prove something about `Fin (n + 1)` where `n` is an
    15в†’arbitrary natural number. The element `x : Fin (n + 1)` has value
    16в†’`x.val` and bound proof `x.isLt : x.val < n + 1`. The goal
    17в†’`x.val в‰¤ n` follows from this bound.
    18в†’
    19в†’The proof uses the same pattern as Level 3: extract the bound
    20в†’with `have h := x.isLt`, then close with `omega`. The bound
    21в†’`x.val < n + 1` implies `x.val в‰¤ n` вЂ” regardless of what `n` is.
    22в†’

    24в†’at most `n`.
    25в†’"
    26в†’
    27в†’/-- Every element of `Fin (n + 1)` has value at most `n`. -/
    28в†’Statement (n : в„•) (x : Fin (n + 1)) : x.val в‰¤ n := by
    29в†’  Hint "Extract the bound proof: `have h := x.isLt`. This gives
    30в†’  `h : x.val < n + 1`."
    31в†’  have h := x.isLt
    32в†’  Hint "Now `{h}` says `x.val < n + 1`, which means `x.val в‰¤ n`.
    33в†’  `omega` handles this conversion."

    35в†’
    36в†’Conclusion "

    38в†’`omega`. The only difference is that `n` is a variable, not a

    40в†’
    41в†’This demonstrates a key point: **all the techniques you've learned
    42в†’generalize beyond concrete types**. Whether the type is `Fin 5`,
    43в†’`Fin 100`, or `Fin (n + 1)` for an unknown `n`:
    44в†’- `.val` extracts the number
    45в†’- `.isLt` gives the bound proof
    46в†’- `Fin.ext_iff` characterizes equality
    47в†’- `omega` handles the arithmetic
    48в†’
    49в†’In later worlds (starting with FinNavigation), you'll work with
    50в†’variable `n` routinely. The moves are the same ones you've
    51в†’practiced here.
    52в†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 11

Title "Beyond Concrete Types"

Introduction "
# From Fin 5 to Fin (n + 1)

Every level so far has used a *concrete* type вЂ” `Fin 5`, `Fin 3`,
`Fin 2`. But the techniques you've learned work for *any* `n`.

Here you'll prove something about `Fin (n + 1)` where `n` is an
arbitrary natural number. The element `x : Fin (n + 1)` has value
`x.val` and bound proof `x.isLt : x.val < n + 1`. The goal
`x.val в‰¤ n` follows from this bound.

The proof uses the same pattern as Level 3: extract the bound
with `have h := x.isLt`, then close with `omega`. The bound
`x.val < n + 1` implies `x.val в‰¤ n` вЂ” regardless of what `n` is.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin (n + 1)` has value
at most `n`.
"

/-- Every element of `Fin (n + 1)` has value at most `n`. -/
Statement (n : в„•) (x : Fin (n + 1)) : x.val в‰¤ n := by
  Hint "Extract the bound proof: `have h := x.isLt`. This gives
  `h : x.val < n + 1`."
  have h := x.isLt
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `x.val < n + 1`, which means `x.val в‰¤ n`.
  `omega` handles this conversion."
  omega

Conclusion "
The proof is identical to Level 3 вЂ” extract `.isLt`, close with
`omega`. The only difference is that `n` is a variable, not a
fixed number.

This demonstrates a key point: **all the techniques you've learned
generalize beyond concrete types**. Whether the type is `Fin 5`,
`Fin 100`, or `Fin (n + 1)` for an unknown `n`:
- `.val` extracts the number
- `.isLt` gives the bound proof
- `Fin.ext_iff` characterizes equality
- `omega` handles the arithmetic

In later worlds (starting with FinNavigation), you'll work with
variable `n` routinely. The moves are the same ones you've
practiced here.
"

1в†’import Game.Metadata
     2в†’
     3в†’World "MeetFin"
     4в†’Level 12
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "The Empty Type"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Fin 0 Has No Elements
    10в†’
    11в†’What is `Fin 0`? It's the type of natural numbers less than 0.
    12в†’But no natural number is less than 0, so **`Fin 0` is empty**.
    13в†’
    14в†’This means: if you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your context, you have
    15в†’a contradiction. From a contradiction, you can prove anything вЂ”
    16в†’including `False`.
    17в†’
    18в†’The key insight: `i.isLt` gives a proof of `i.val < 0`, but no
    19в†’natural number satisfies this. So `omega` can derive `False` from
    20в†’the contradictory hypothesis.
    21в†’
    22в†’**Your task**: Given `i : Fin 0`, prove `False`.
    23в†’
    24в†’Strategy: extract the impossible bound with `have h := i.isLt`,
    25в†’then close with `omega`.
    26в†’"
    27в†’
    28в†’/-- An element of `Fin 0` leads to a contradiction. -/
    29в†’Statement (i : Fin 0) : False := by
    30в†’  Hint "Extract the bound proof: `have h := i.isLt`. This gives
    31в†’  you `h : i.val < 0`, which is impossible for a natural number."
    32в†’  have h := i.isLt
    33в†’  Hint "Now `{h}` says a natural number is less than 0. `omega` can
    34в†’  see this is impossible."
    35в†’  omega
    36в†’
    37в†’Conclusion "
    38в†’`Fin 0` is the **empty type** вЂ” it has no elements at all.
    39в†’
    40в†’If you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your hypotheses, you're in a
    41в†’contradictory context. The `.isLt` field gives `i.val < 0`, and
    42в†’since no natural number is less than 0, `omega` derives `False`.
    43в†’
    44в†’This pattern вЂ” extract `.isLt`, close with `omega` вЂ” is the **Fin
    45в†’contradiction pattern**. You already used it in Level 3 (where
    46в†’`x.val = 5` contradicted `x.val < 5`). You'll use it again in the
    47в†’case analysis levels ahead, where impossible branches have a value
    48в†’too large for the bound.
    49в†’
    50в†’This is an important edge case to keep in mind: `Fin n` for `n = 0`
    51в†’is qualitatively different from `Fin n` for `n в‰Ґ 1`.
    52в†’
    53в†’*Alternative approach*: `exact Fin.elim0 i` also works вЂ” it's a
    54в†’built-in function that eliminates any `Fin 0` element.
    55в†’"
    56в†’
    57в†’DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
    58в†’

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 12

Title "The Empty Type"

Introduction "
# Fin 0 Has No Elements

What is `Fin 0`? It's the type of natural numbers less than 0.
But no natural number is less than 0, so **`Fin 0` is empty**.

This means: if you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your context, you have
a contradiction. From a contradiction, you can prove anything вЂ”
including `False`.

The key insight: `i.isLt` gives a proof of `i.val < 0`, but no
natural number satisfies this. So `omega` can derive `False` from
the contradictory hypothesis.

**Your task**: Given `i : Fin 0`, prove `False`.

Strategy: extract the impossible bound with `have h := i.isLt`,
then close with `omega`.
"

/-- An element of `Fin 0` leads to a contradiction. -/
Statement (i : Fin 0) : False := by
  Hint "Extract the bound proof: `have h := i.isLt`. This gives
  you `h : i.val < 0`, which is impossible for a natural number."
  have h := i.isLt
  Hint "Now `{h}` says a natural number is less than 0. `omega` can
  see this is impossible."
  omega

Conclusion "
`Fin 0` is the **empty type** вЂ” it has no elements at all.

If you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your hypotheses, you're in a
contradictory context. The `.isLt` field gives `i.val < 0`, and
since no natural number is less than 0, `omega` derives `False`.

This pattern вЂ” extract `.isLt`, close with `omega` вЂ” is the **Fin
contradiction pattern**. You already used it in Level 3 (where
`x.val = 5` contradicted `x.val < 5`). You'll use it again in the
case analysis levels ahead, where impossible branches have a value
too large for the bound.

This is an important edge case to keep in mind: `Fin n` for `n = 0`
is qualitatively different from `Fin n` for `n в‰Ґ 1`.

*Alternative approach*: `exact Fin.elim0 i` also works вЂ” it's a
built-in function that eliminates any `Fin 0` element.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 13

Title "From Nothing, Everything"

Introduction "
# The Explosion Principle

In Level 12, you proved that `Fin 0` leads to `False`. But why is
that useful? Because from `False`, you can prove **anything**.

This is the *explosion principle* (ex falso quodlibet): a false
hypothesis implies every statement. If your context contains a
contradiction, every goal is provable.

Here the goal is `42 = 0` вЂ” obviously false in normal mathematics.
But you have `i : Fin 0`, which is a contradiction (no natural
number is less than 0). From this contradictory hypothesis, even
`42 = 0` follows.

**Strategy**: Extract the contradiction from `i` the same way
you did in Level 11 (`have h := i.isLt` then `omega`), and `omega`
will close the goal вЂ” it sees that the hypotheses are contradictory
and doesn't even need to engage with `42 = 0`.
"

/-- From an element of the empty type, anything follows. -/
Statement (i : Fin 0) : 42 = 0 := by
  Hint "Extract the bound: `have h := i.isLt`. This gives a proof of
  `i.val < 0`, which is impossible."
  have h := i.isLt
  Hint "Now `{h}` says a natural number is less than 0. `omega` sees
  this is contradictory and closes any goal."
  omega

Conclusion "
This is the **explosion principle**: from a contradiction, you can
prove anything. Having `i : Fin 0` is a contradiction because no
natural number is less than 0.

The pattern is the same as Level 12 вЂ” the **Fin contradiction pattern** (`have h := i.isLt; omega`) вЂ”
but now the goal is an arbitrary false statement instead of `False`
itself. The key insight: `omega` doesn't just prove `False` from
contradictory hypotheses; it closes *any* goal when the hypotheses
are contradictory.

You'll encounter this principle throughout mathematics: whenever
you can show a hypothesis is impossible, the entire branch of
reasoning collapses and every statement in that branch is provable.

*Shortcut*: `exact Fin.elim0 i` does the same thing in one step.
It's the canonical way experienced Lean users handle `Fin 0`.
"

/-- `Fin.elim0` eliminates an element of `Fin 0`. Since `Fin 0` is
empty, this can produce a value of any type.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.elim0 i
```
where `i : Fin 0`.

## When to use it
When you have `i : Fin 0` in context. Since `Fin 0` has no elements,
`i` is a contradiction, and `Fin.elim0 i` can produce any type.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.elim0 as "Fin.elim0"

NewDefinition Fin.elim0

1в†’import Game.Metadata
     2в†’
     3в†’World "MeetFin"
     4в†’Level 14
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "The Unique Element"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Fin 1 Has Exactly One Element
    10в†’
    11в†’`Fin 1` is the type of natural numbers less than 1. The only such
    12в†’number is 0, so `Fin 1` has **exactly one element**.
    13в†’
    14в†’This means every `x : Fin 1` must equal `0`. To prove this, we'll
    15в†’introduce a new tactic: **`ext`**.
    16в†’
    17в†’The `ext` tactic is shorthand for `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` вЂ” it converts
    18в†’a goal `a = b` (for `Fin` elements) into `a.val = b.val`. The name
    19в†’stands for *extensionality*: two structured values are equal when
    20в†’their components are equal.
    21в†’
    22в†’`ext` is a **general-purpose tactic** вЂ” it works not just for `Fin`,
    23в†’but for any type with an `@[ext]` attribute (functions, sets, and
    24в†’many other types you'll meet later). Learning it here on `Fin` will
    25в†’pay off when you encounter function extensionality in later worlds.
    26в†’
    27в†’**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 1` equals `0`.
    28в†’Use `ext` then `omega`.
    29в†’"
    30в†’
    31в†’/-- `Fin 1` has exactly one element: `0`. -/
    32в†’Statement (x : Fin 1) : x = 0 := by
    33в†’  Hint "Use `ext` to convert the `Fin` equality to a value equality.
    34в†’  This is the same as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` but shorter."
    35в†’  ext
    36в†’  Hint "Now the goal is `x.val = 0`. Since `x : Fin 1`, you know
    37в†’  `x.val < 1`, so `x.val = 0`. `omega` handles this."
    38в†’  omega
    39в†’
    40в†’Conclusion "
    41в†’`Fin 1` is a **singleton type** вЂ” it has exactly one element, which
    42в†’is `0`.
    43в†’
    44в†’The `ext` tactic did the same work as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`: it reduced
    45в†’`Fin` equality to value equality. From now on, you can use either вЂ”
    46в†’`ext` is shorter, `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` is more explicit.
    47в†’
    48в†’This is the **Fin equality pattern** from Level 10: reduce to values,
    49в†’then compute. Whether you write `ext; omega` or
    50в†’`rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`, the effect is the same.
    51в†’
    52в†’| `Fin n` | Number of elements | Character |
    53в†’|---|---|---|
    54в†’| `Fin 0` | 0 | Empty |
    55в†’| `Fin 1` | 1 | Singleton |
    56в†’| `Fin 2` | 2 | Coming soon! |
    57в†’"
    58в†’
    59в†’/-- `ext` proves equality of structured types by reducing to equality
    60в†’of their components.
    61в†’
    62в†’## Syntax
    63в†’```
    64в†’ext
    65в†’```
    66в†’
    67в†’## When to use it
    68в†’When the goal is `a = b` where the type has an `@[ext]` attribute.
    69в†’
    70в†’For `Fin n`: converts `a = b` to `a.val = b.val`.
    71в†’For functions: converts `f = g` to `в€Ђ x, f x = g x` (called `funext`).
    72в†’
    73в†’## Example
    74в†’```
    75в†’-- Goal: x = 0  (where x : Fin 1)
    76в†’ext
    77в†’-- Goal: x.val = (0 : Fin 1).val
    78в†’```
    79в†’
    80в†’`ext` is a general-purpose tactic that works for many types beyond
    81в†’`Fin`. It replaces `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for Fin equality goals.
    82в†’-/
    83в†’TacticDoc ext
    84в†’
    85в†’NewTactic ext

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 14

Title "The Unique Element"

Introduction "
# Fin 1 Has Exactly One Element

`Fin 1` is the type of natural numbers less than 1. The only such
number is 0, so `Fin 1` has **exactly one element**.

This means every `x : Fin 1` must equal `0`. To prove this, we'll
introduce a new tactic: **`ext`**.

The `ext` tactic is shorthand for `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` вЂ” it converts
a goal `a = b` (for `Fin` elements) into `a.val = b.val`. The name
stands for *extensionality*: two structured values are equal when
their components are equal.

`ext` is a **general-purpose tactic** вЂ” it works not just for `Fin`,
but for any type with an `@[ext]` attribute (functions, sets, and
many other types you'll meet later). Learning it here on `Fin` will
pay off when you encounter function extensionality in later worlds.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 1` equals `0`.
Use `ext` then `omega`.
"

/-- `Fin 1` has exactly one element: `0`. -/
Statement (x : Fin 1) : x = 0 := by
  Hint "Use `ext` to convert the `Fin` equality to a value equality.
  This is the same as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` but shorter."
  ext
  Hint "Now the goal is `x.val = 0`. Since `x : Fin 1`, you know
  `x.val < 1`, so `x.val = 0`. `omega` handles this."
  omega

Conclusion "
`Fin 1` is a **singleton type** вЂ” it has exactly one element, which
is `0`.

The `ext` tactic did the same work as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`: it reduced
`Fin` equality to value equality. From now on, you can use either вЂ”
`ext` is shorter, `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` is more explicit.

This is the **Fin equality pattern** from Level 10: reduce to values,
then compute. Whether you write `ext; omega` or
`rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`, the effect is the same.

| `Fin n` | Number of elements | Character |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin 0` | 0 | Empty |
| `Fin 1` | 1 | Singleton |
| `Fin 2` | 2 | Coming soon! |
"

/-- `ext` proves equality of structured types by reducing to equality
of their components.

## Syntax
```
ext
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `a = b` where the type has an `@[ext]` attribute.

For `Fin n`: converts `a = b` to `a.val = b.val`.
For functions: converts `f = g` to `в€Ђ x, f x = g x` (called `funext`).

## Example
```
-- Goal: x = 0  (where x : Fin 1)
ext
-- Goal: x.val = (0 : Fin 1).val
```

`ext` is a general-purpose tactic that works for many types beyond
`Fin`. It replaces `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for Fin equality goals.
-/
TacticDoc ext

NewTactic ext

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
DisabledTheorem Unique.eq_default Subsingleton.elim

1в†’import Game.Metadata
     2в†’
     3в†’World "MeetFin"
     4в†’Level 15
     5в†’
     6в†’Title "Destructuring Fin"
     7в†’
     8в†’Introduction "
     9в†’# Destructuring Fin Elements
    10в†’
    11в†’So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
    12в†’notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
    13в†’**destructuring** with `cases`.
    14в†’
    15в†’When you write:
    16в†’```
    17в†’cases x with | mk v hlt =>
    18в†’```
    19в†’Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
    20в†’- `v : в„•` вЂ” the underlying value
    21в†’- `hlt : v < 3` вЂ” the bound proof
    22в†’
    23в†’This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
    24в†’standalone variables in your context.
    25в†’
    26в†’Why learn a second way to access these? Because in the next level,
    27в†’you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** вЂ” asking whether
    28в†’`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
    29в†’variable, which destructuring provides.
    30в†’
    31в†’**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` can be reconstructed
    32в†’from a natural number and a bound proof. In other words, show there
    33в†’exist `v` and `h : v < 3` such that `x = вџЁv, hвџ©`.
    34в†’
    35в†’Destructure `x` first вЂ” after that, the components are right there in
    36в†’your context and you can package them up.
    37в†’"
    38в†’
    39в†’/-- Every Fin element can be reconstructed from its components. -/
    40в†’Statement (x : Fin 3) : в€ѓ (v : в„•) (h : v < 3), x = вџЁv, hвџ© := by
    41в†’  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
    42в†’  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
    43в†’  cases x with
    44в†’  | mk v hlt =>
    45в†’    Hint "Now you have `v : в„•` and `hlt : v < 3` as standalone
    46в†’    variables. The goal asks for a number and a bound proof such that
    47в†’    the element equals the reconstruction. You already have both!
    48в†’    Use `exact вџЁv, hlt, rflвџ©` to provide `v` as the number, `hlt`
    49в†’    as the bound proof, and `rfl` for the equality (since the element
    50в†’    IS its own reconstruction)."
    51в†’    exact вџЁv, hlt, rflвџ©
    52в†’
    53в†’Conclusion "
    54в†’You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.
    55в†’
    56в†’The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
    57в†’a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
    58в†’apart with `cases` to expose its components.
    59в†’
    60в†’| Before destructuring | After |
    61в†’|---|---|
    62в†’| `x : Fin 3` | `v : в„•`, `hlt : v < 3` |
    63в†’| `x.val` | `v` |
    64в†’| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |
    65в†’
    66в†’After destructuring, the components are standalone variables that
    67в†’you can use directly вЂ” as existential witnesses, in case splits, or
    68в†’in any other tactic that needs a named term. The equality
    69в†’`вџЁv, hltвџ© = вџЁv, hltвџ©` closes by `rfl` because the element IS its
    70в†’own reconstruction from components.
    71в†’
    72в†’You'll need destructuring in the next level for full case analysis.
    73в†’"
    74в†’
    75в†’DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases
    76в†’
